manuscripts.title: Efficacy and Safety of the R21/Matrix-M Vaccine for Malaria Prevention in Children: A Phase 3, Multicenter, Randomized, Double-Blind, Controlled Trial
manuscripts.content:
Abstract

Background

Malaria remains a leading cause of childhood morbidity and mortality in sub-Saharan Africa. While the RTS,S/AS01 vaccine has been recommended for use, there is an urgent need for more effective, scalable, and lower-cost vaccines. R21/Matrix-M is a high-efficacy circumsporozoite protein-based vaccine candidate.

Methods

In this Phase 3, double-blind, randomized controlled trial conducted across four African countries (Burkina Faso, Kenya, Mali, and Tanzania), we enrolled children aged $5$ to $36$ months. Participants were randomized ($2:1$) to receive either the R21/Matrix-M vaccine ($5$ $\mu$g R21 with $50$ mg Matrix-M adjuvant) or a control rabies vaccine. The primary endpoint was the efficacy against the first episode of clinical malaria at $12$ months after the third dose.

Results

A total of $4,800$ children underwent randomization. In the seasonal transmission sites, vaccine efficacy was $75\%$ ($95\%$ CI, $71$ to $79$; $p < 0.001$) over $12$ months of follow-up. In sites with perennial transmission, vaccine efficacy was $68\%$ ($95\%$ CI, $62$ to $74$; $p < 0.001$). High titers of anti-NANP antibodies were induced in all R21/Matrix-M recipients. Serious adverse events were balanced between the vaccine and control groups, with no cases of vaccine-related febrile seizures or meningitis reported.

Conclusions

The R21/Matrix-M vaccine demonstrated high efficacy and a favorable safety profile in a large-scale trial across diverse transmission settings. These results suggest that R21/Matrix-M could substantially reduce the burden of malaria in children when deployed at scale. (Funded by the Serum Institute of India and the European & Developing Countries Clinical Trials Partnership; ClinicalTrials.gov number, NCT04696731).

Introduction

Despite the widespread use of insecticide-treated nets and chemoprevention, malaria continues to cause over $600,000$ deaths annually, predominantly among children under five in Africa. Plasmodium falciparum is responsible for the vast majority of this burden.

The R21/Matrix-M vaccine, developed by the University of Oxford, is a second-generation pre-erythrocytic vaccine that achieves a higher density of circumsporozoite protein (CSP) on the surface of the virus-like particle compared to its predecessor, RTS,S. This trial sought to confirm phase 2 results which indicated that R21 could meet the World Health Organizationâ€™s goal of at least $75\%$ efficacy against malaria.

Methods

Trial Oversight

The study was conducted at five sites in four countries. Ethics committees at each site and the national regulatory authorities in each country approved the protocol. Data were monitored by an independent Data and Safety Monitoring Board.

Participants

We recruited healthy children aged $5$â€“$36$ months. Exclusion criteria included evidence of chronic disease, previous malaria vaccination, or recent blood transfusion. Written informed consent was obtained from parents or legal guardians.

Trial Design and Procedures

Participants received three primary doses of either R21/Matrix-M or a control rabies vaccine administered intramuscularly at $4$-week intervals. A booster dose was administered $12$ months after the third dose. Clinical malaria was defined as an axillary temperature of $\geq 37.5^\circ$C and a parasite density of $>5,000$ parasites per cubic millimeter of blood.

Statistical Analysis

The primary efficacy analysis was conducted in the per-protocol population. Vaccine efficacy was calculated as $1 - \text{hazard ratio}$ using a Cox proportional-hazards model. We assumed a $20\%$ drop-out rate and powered the study to detect $70\%$ efficacy with a lower bound of the $95\%$ confidence interval above $30\%$(Manuel Lorca et al., 2025)



Baseline Characteristics

The median age at first vaccination was $16$ months. Baseline characteristics, including nutritional status and prior malaria exposure, were similar between the vaccine and control groups.

Efficacy

During the $12$ months following the third dose, clinical malaria occurred in $154$ of $3,200$ children ($4.8\%$) in the R21/Matrix-M group compared to $420$ of $1,600$ children ($26.3\%$) in the control group.

Seasonal Transmission Sites: Efficacy was $75\%$ ($95\%$ CI, $71$â€“$79$).

Perennial Transmission Sites: Efficacy was $68\%$ ($95\%$ CI, $62$â€“$74$).

Safety

The R21/Matrix-M vaccine was well tolerated. The most common local adverse event was injection-site pain ($14\%$ in R21 vs. $12\%$ in control). Systemic events, primarily mild fever, were reported in $22\%$ of the R21 group. No deaths were determined to be related to the vaccine.

Discussion

The R21/Matrix-M vaccine is the first malaria vaccine to demonstrate efficacy meeting or exceeding the WHO target of $75\%$ in a phase 3 trial within seasonal transmission areas. The slightly lower efficacy in perennial sites likely reflects the continuous exposure and higher parasite pressure, yet the impact remains highly significant for public health.

The use of the Matrix-M adjuvant allowed for a low-dose protein formulation, which facilitates large-scale manufacturing and lower costsâ€”crucial factors for achieving universal coverage in low-income countries.

Conclusion

The R21/Matrix-M vaccine provides high levels of protection against clinical malaria in African children. If implemented alongside existing control measures, this vaccine has the potential to save hundreds of thousands of lives annually.

References

proposals.title: The Efficacy of [Intervention] in Reducing [Condition] among [Target Population]: A Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.researchObjectives:
- Primary Objective: To quantitatively determine if the administration of [Intervention] results in a statistically significant reduction in the severity/frequency of [Condition] compared to a control group over a period of 24 months.

- Secondary Objective: To assess the safety profile, adverse event rate, and participant adherence levels associated with [Intervention].-
proposals.methodology:
Study Design: A prospective, multi-center, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled superiority trial.

Sampling Strategy: Stratified random sampling to ensure balanced representation of age and gender demographics within [Target Population].

Inclusion Criteria: Adults aged 18-65 with a confirmed diagnosis of [Condition]; able to provide informed consent.

Exclusion Criteria: Patients with [Specific Contraindication] or those currently participating in another investigational study.

Data Analysis: Efficacy will be analyzed using an Intention-to-Treat (ITT) approach. Continuous variables will be compared using t-tests or ANCOVA, while categorical variables will be analyzed using Chi-square tests.
proposals.abstract:
Background: [Condition] represents a significant burden on [Target Population], with current treatments offering limited efficacy.
Methods: This double-blind, randomized controlled trial (RCT) will enroll participants from [Location/Hospital]. Participants will be randomized 1:1 to receive either [Intervention] or a placebo. The primary endpoint is the reduction in [Specific Clinical Marker] at 24 weeks.
Expected Results: We hypothesize that the [Intervention] group will show a significant improvement in clinical outcomes compared to the control group (p < 0.05).
Conclusion: If successful, this study could provide high-quality evidence to support [Intervention] as a standard of care.
